original_hypothesis.title: BurnToMint Bypasses Max Purchases Limit
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The burnToMint function does not check the maxCollectionPurchases limit. A user who acquires many burnable tokens from an older collection can burn them to mint an unlimited number of tokens in a new collection, bypassing the public mint limits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Public mint limits are designed to ensure fair distribution and prevent whales from hoarding a new collection. By bypassing this limit via the burn mechanism, a whale can corner the market on a new collection, manipulating secondary market dynamics.
preconditions: burnToMint is initialized between an old and new collection
Attacker holds more old tokens than the new collection's max allowance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: tokensMintedPerAddress is not incremented or checked during burnToMint
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker hoards the new collection, creating artificial scarcity and flipping them at a premium.

economic_questions: Is this bypass an intentional design choice for burn mechanics?
What is the cost of acquiring burnable tokens vs the expected premium of the new collection?
validation_requirements: Test burning more tokens than maxCollectionPurchases

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to acquire burnable tokens + minting fees
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Secondary market premium of hoarded tokens

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient supply of old tokens on secondary markets
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Centralization of supply, harming community distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Honest minters who are locked out by supply caps
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Market cap of the new collection

validation_result.mechanism: per-address mint limit bypass via burnToMint
validation_result.impact_summary: The burnToMint function bypasses the maxCollectionPurchases per-address limit enforced in the regular mint function. A user holding many burnable tokens can mint an unlimited number of tokens in the destination collection, circumventing the fair distribution mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The regular mint() function enforces a per-address limit via two checks during the public phase: (1) `require(_numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col))` and (2) `require(gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, msg.sender) + _numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col))`. Additionally, NextGenCore.mint() increments `tokensMintedPerAddress[_collectionID][_mintingAddress]` to track cumulative mints per address. In contrast, MinterContract.burnToMint() performs none of these checks. It does not call viewMaxAllowance, does not check retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress, and NextGenCore.burnToMint() does not increment tokensMintedPerAddress. This means a single address can call burnToMint repeatedly (once per burnable token held) without ever hitting the maxCollectionPurchases cap that applies to public minting.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. MinterContract.burnToMint (approximately lines 258-272) lacks any reference to maxCollectionPurchases or tokensMintedPerAddress. Comparing with mint() (lines ~196-242) which has explicit checks `require(_numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col))` and `require(gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(col, msg.sender) + _numberOfTokens <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(col))`, the omission is clear. NextGenCore.burnToMint() also does not increment tokensMintedPerAddress, unlike NextGenCore.mint() which does.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check on gencore.viewMaxAllowance(_mintCollectionID) and no tracking of tokens minted per address. Compare with mint() which enforces both checks during public phase.

Unlike NextGenCore.mint() which increments tokensMintedPerAddress[_collectionID][_mintingAddress] for phase 2 mints, burnToMint does not increment any per-address counter, making the bypass invisible to subsequent maxAllowance checks.

This is the correct enforcement in mint() that is missing from burnToMint(). Shows that maxCollectionPurchases enforcement was intended for public minting but was not applied to the burn-to-mint path.

calling_sequence: 2. Function checks burnToMintCollections mapping, time window, token ID range, supply, and ETH value
3. No check on maxCollectionPurchases or tokensMintedPerAddress is performed
4. Function calls gencore.burnToMint() which burns the old token and mints new one
5. NextGenCore.burnToMint() increments collectionCirculationSupply but does NOT increment tokensMintedPerAddress
6. User repeats with another burnable token - no per-address limit prevents this
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: MinterContract.burnToMint -> NextGenCore.burnToMint. Neither function checks or increments the per-address public mint counter (tokensMintedPerAddress). The maxCollectionPurchases limit stored in collectionAdditionalData[col].maxCollectionPurchases (accessible via viewMaxAllowance) is never consulted. A user with N burnable tokens can call burnToMint N times to accumulate N tokens in the mint collection, regardless of maxCollectionPurchases being set to, say, 1 or 2.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure burn-to-mint relationships. The fixturesDeployment.js only sets up basic contracts without initializing burn mechanics.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. Admin creates collection A (old) with 100 supply and collection B (new) with 100 supply and maxCollectionPurchases = 2. 2. Admin calls initializeBurn(collectionA, collectionB, true) to enable burn-to-mint. 3. Attacker acquires 50 tokens from collection A on secondary market. 4. During collection B's public sale, honest users can only mint 2 tokens each via mint(). 5. Attacker calls burnToMint 50 times, minting 50 tokens in collection B despite maxCollectionPurchases being 2. 6. Attacker now holds 50/100 of collection B's supply while honest users are limited to 2 each.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The maxCollectionPurchases limit is a critical fair distribution mechanism. Bypassing it allows a single address to accumulate a disproportionate share of a new collection. While the attack requires acquiring burnable tokens (non-zero cost), the per-address limit is completely unenforced, which is a definitive code defect in the access control/limit enforcement logic.
recommended_mitigations: Add maxCollectionPurchases check in MinterContract.burnToMint: require(gencore.retrieveTokensMintedPublicPerAddress(_mintCollectionID, msg.sender) + 1 <= gencore.viewMaxAllowance(_mintCollectionID), 'Max')
Increment tokensMintedPerAddress in NextGenCore.burnToMint similar to how NextGenCore.mint does for phase 2
Alternatively, add a separate per-address burn-to-mint counter and limit if different limits are desired for burn mechanics vs public mint
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts using fixturesDeployment.js setup. 2. Create two collections (A and B) via createCollection and setCollectionData with maxCollectionPurchases=2 for collection B. 3. Set costs and phases for both. 4. Call initializeBurn(A, B, true). 5. Mint 10 tokens in collection A to attacker address. 6. During collection B's public phase, call burnToMint 10 times from attacker address. 7. Verify attacker holds 10 tokens in collection B despite maxCollectionPurchases=2. 8. Verify that calling mint() from same address would fail after 2 tokens.
uncertainties: Whether this omission is an intentional design choice (burn mechanics may be considered a different distribution channel with its own natural limits via burn token supply). However, there is no documentation or code comment suggesting this is intentional.
additional_testing_needed: Test burnToMint with maxCollectionPurchases set to 1 and verify more than 1 can be minted via burn path
Test that tokensMintedPerAddress counter remains 0 after multiple burnToMint calls
Test that the same address can still mint via regular mint() after exhausting burn tokens (counters are independent)
